moveAnnotations.annotation: We're launching the Nimzo-Larsen Attack, an offbeat yet solid opening that prepares to fianchetto our light-squared bishop on b2. By playing b3 first, we avoid the theoretical mainlines while still controlling the critical central squares (especially e4 and d4) and preparing a flexible setup. This move has the advantage of keeping Black guessing about our exact plans while we build a harmonious pawn structure.

Black immediately stakes a claim in the center, which is natural and principled—Black doesn't want to passively allow us to control d4 and e4 uncontested. This move supports Black's development and opens lines for the queenside pieces, though it does commit the pawn and can be met with various strategic plans on our side.

This is the Graz Attack, where we aggressively develop our bishop to a3 to pin or pressure Black's pieces along the long diagonal. We're targeting b4 and potentially c5, while simultaneously supporting our light-squared strategy and preventing Black's bishop from comfortably settling on e6 or f5. The move is unconventional and puts immediate psychological pressure on Black—we're not playing for small advantages, but rather trying to dictate the game's character early on.
